name: Petilla de Aragon — round trip from Uncastillo
name_original: Petilla de Aragón — ida y vuelta desde Uncastillo
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Uncastillo traverse a diverse landscape at the entrance to the Pre-Pyrenees. The region features a blend of semi-desert areas, riverine environments, and Aleppo pine forests. Cyclists encounter varied terrain, including ravines, plateaus, and hills, providing both challenging ascents and enjoyable descents. The area is characterized by its historical sites and natural vistas, offering a dynamic backdrop for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Uncastillo The most popular road cycling route is Embalse de Yesa — circular of Sos del Rey Católico, a 50.3 miles (80.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 52 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers extensive views around the Yesa reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puerto de Sos and Castiliscar Loop, a moderate 28.6 miles (46.0 km) path. This loop includes a mountain pass, providing varied terrain and scenic vistas. Local road cyclists also love the Sádaba and Uncastillo Castles Loop, a 27.8 miles (44.7 km) trail leading through historical towns and past castles, often completed in about 2 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling around Uncastillo is defined by its Pre-Pyrenean hills, riverine environments, and historical landmarks. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Uncastillo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from a few reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Uncastillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Uncastillo?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes around Uncastillo, offering a variety of experiences. These routes cater to different skill levels, from moderate to difficult, ensuring there's something for every road cyclist.

Q: What is the typical terrain like for road cycling in Uncastillo?
A: Road cycling in Uncastillo offers a diverse terrain, characteristic of the Pre-Pyrenees. You'll encounter a blend of semi-desert areas, riverine environments, and Aleppo pine forests. The routes feature varied topography, including ravines, plateaus, and hills, providing both challenging ascents and enjoyable descents.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes in Uncastillo?
A: Yes, Uncastillo offers several challenging road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992899248/petilla-de-aragon-round-trip-from-uncastillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Petilla de Aragon — round trip from Uncastillo&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 83.9 km route with over 1,400 meters of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992890036/embalse-de-yesa-circular-of-sos-del-rey-catolico" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Yesa — circular of Sos del Rey Católico&lt;/a&gt;, a 80.9 km route with extensive views and significant climbs.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling options for beginners?
A: While many routes feature varied terrain, some are more moderate. The region's network includes options suitable for those looking for less strenuous rides. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992763037/sadaba-and-uncastillo-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sádaba and Uncastillo Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 44.7 km trail that leads through historical towns and past castles, offering a rewarding experience without extreme difficulty.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see while road cycling near Uncastillo?
A: The Uncastillo area is rich in history. You can cycle past numerous Romanesque churches within Uncastillo itself, and the town is a declared Historic-Artistic Site. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992763037/sadaba-and-uncastillo-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sádaba and Uncastillo Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically takes you past historical towns and castles. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5314821" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Bañales Roman Archaeological Site&lt;/a&gt;, with its remnants of a forum, baths, and aqueduct, is about 15 kilometers from Uncastillo and makes for a fascinating detour.

Q: Are there natural features or scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The region offers stunning natural vistas. You can find scenic viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5286112" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de las Ripas Altas&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5284678" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puy Fonguera Summit and Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992890036/embalse-de-yesa-circular-of-sos-del-rey-catolico" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Yesa — circular of Sos del Rey Católico&lt;/a&gt; route provides extensive views around the Yesa reservoir, showcasing the area's riverine environments and diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes starting from Uncastillo?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops, often starting and ending in or near Uncastillo or other nearby towns. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992763037/sadaba-and-uncastillo-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sádaba and Uncastillo Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular route. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990790948/puerto-de-sos-and-castiliscar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Sos and Castiliscar Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which includes a mountain pass and varied terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Uncastillo?
A: The diverse landscapes of Uncastillo, from semi-desert areas to riverine environments, make it suitable for cycling across various seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures for road cycling. Summers can be warm, but routes through forested or riverine areas provide some respite. Winter cycling is possible, but checking local weather conditions is always advisable.

Q: Is Uncastillo suitable for family-friendly road cycling?
A: While many routes involve varied terrain and elevation changes, some sections or shorter, flatter roads within the region might be suitable for families with older children or those comfortable with moderate distances. For very young children, dedicated cycling paths might be limited, but the area's low-traffic secondary roads can be an option for experienced family cyclists.

Q: Can I bring my dog on road cycling routes in Uncastillo?
A: Road cycling routes typically follow paved roads and are generally not ideal for dogs, especially for longer distances or at higher speeds due to safety concerns and the dog's physical exertion. If you wish to explore with your dog, consider alternative activities like hiking on suitable trails, where conditions are safer and more comfortable for pets.

Q: Where can cyclists find parking in Uncastillo?
A: Uncastillo, as a historic town, offers various parking options, often in designated areas on the outskirts of the medieval center. Look for public parking lots or street parking available for visitors. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival for the most current information on parking regulations and availability for cyclists.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the routes?
A: Yes, Uncastillo and the surrounding towns along the routes offer opportunities for refreshments. Uncastillo itself has cafes and restaurants where you can refuel. Routes that pass through other villages like Sos del Rey Católico or Sádaba will also provide options for food and drinks, allowing you to experience local cuisine during your ride.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Uncastillo?
A: The road cycling routes around Uncastillo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 400 cyclists. Reviewers often praise the varied terrain, the blend of historical sites and natural beauty, and the challenging yet rewarding ascents and descents that characterize the Pre-Pyrenean landscape.

Q: How can I reach Uncastillo using public transport for cycling?
A: Public transport options to Uncastillo might be limited, as it is a smaller, historic town. While there may be regional bus services connecting it to larger cities in Aragon, these services often have restrictions on carrying bicycles. It's recommended to check specific bus company policies and schedules in advance if you plan to use public transport with your bike.
tips:
Uncastillo, located in the region of Cinco Villas in Aragon, is by the extent of its historical and architectural heritage a real treasure in itself.

Around Uncastillo Castle, there are no less than six churches. They all date from a different era.

The Church of San Martin in Tours, with its magnificent Romanesque porch and its expressive characters, clearly dates from the Romanesque period. The Church of San Andrés, with its colonnades and its coat of arms, is more from the Renaissance period.

Don't miss, finally, the museum of the Tower. If you are tired of religious monuments, do not hesitate to get lost in the many streets of the village.

Despite its curious and striking name, this town is serious when it comes to history and places to visit. Here is the Palace of Sada where King Ferdinand II of Aragon, known as the "Catholic", was born, a place worth visiting to understand the history of Spain. It is also very beautiful to walk its streets and appreciate its stone houses and discover some corner with beautiful panoramic views.

We are in the curious town of Petilla de Aragón, an exclave of Navarra and an enclave in the province of Zaragoza (completely surrounded by the territory of Zaragoza). We will access it from the road between Navardún and Sos del Rey Católico.
As a curiosity, the doctor Santiago Ramón y Cajal, Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1906, was born here.
As religious heritage it has the hermitage of San Antonio or the church of San Millán
